items.title: Inside Banana RAT: From Build Server to Banking Fraud
items.description: An MDR investigation successfully mapped the complete operational infrastructure of Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan operated by threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063. The investigation uncovered both server-side and client-side components, revealing a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system that produces hash-unique builds to evade detection. The malware employs layered
items.ai_summary: The Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan, has been found to employ a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system, producing hash-unique builds to evade detection. This malware operates by exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by the T1190 TTP, and impairing defenses, as seen in T1562. The real-world impact of this threat includes the potential for banking fraud, with the threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063 being attributed to its operation. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust web application firewalls and ensure that all public-facing applications are up-to-date and patched, specifically focusing on securing API endpoints. Additionally, network traffic monitoring should be enhanced to detect and block suspicious activity related to the FastAPI-based payload generation system.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1562| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest PyPi Compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client compromised versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3 on PyPi. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account previously linked to attacks, using stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens. The evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, Vault,
items.ai_summary: The latest PyPi compromise is a supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client, specifically versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account, utilizing stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens, which is an example of T1078 (Valid Accounts) and T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise). This evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from cloud providers such as AWS, Azure, GCP, as well as Kubernetes and Vault, highlighting the potential for widespread impact. To remediate, defenders should immediately update the Microsoft DurableTask Python client to a version outside the affected range (1.4.1-1.4.3) and review GitHub account security, ensuring that all credentials are secure and that two-factor authentication is enabled. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious activity related to the stolen credentials is crucial, such as unusual login attempts from unfamiliar locations or devices.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1195| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of Gamaredon campaign targeting Ukraine weaponizing CVE-2025-8088
items.description: A campaign exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability CVE-2025-8088 has been actively targeting Ukraine since February 2026, with ongoing activity through June 2026. The operation uses Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures, distributed as RAR archives. The malicious archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences that automatically place L
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon campaign has been exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability, leveraging Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures to target Ukraine since February 2026. The malicious RAR archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences, which automatically place malware on the victim's system, utilizing T1204 user execution and T1564.004 NTFS file attributes tactics. If exploited, this vulnerability allows attackers to encrypt data for impact, as seen in T1486, and potentially collect sensitive information, as indicated by T1560 archive collected data. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are using the latest version of WinRAR and implement robust email filtering to block malicious RAR archives, specifically those with NTFS alternate data streams.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1564.004| C[NTFS File Attributes]:::tactic
    C -->|T1560| D[Archive Collected Data]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OXLOADER: new loader evading detection to drop infostealer
items.description: A previously undocumented Windows loader designated as OXLOADER delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving remarkably low detection rates. The loader employs multiple obfuscation layers including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques, along with self-modifying decryption stubs and abuse of the Windows .relo
items.ai_summary: The OXLOADER is a previously undocumented Windows loader that delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving low detection rates due to its multiple obfuscation layers, including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques. This loader affects Windows systems, with the exact version not specified, and can lead to significant real-world impact, such as data breaches and financial loss, as the CASTLESTEALER infostealer can steal sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust anti-malware solutions, ensure regular system updates, and utilize intrusion detection systems to identify and block suspicious activity. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on Google Ads and verify the authenticity of the advertisements. The threat actor behind OXLOADER is not explicitly mentioned, but their tactics suggest a sophisticated and well-resourced group.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation FlutterBridge: The FlutterShell macOS Backdoor
items.description: FlutterShell is a macOS backdoor campaign active from December 2025 to March 2026, identified as cluster CL-CRI-1089 under Operation FlutterBridge. The threat actors deliberately misused the Flutter framework to deliver malware through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube. The malware employs a two-component architecture: a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib. Across thr
items.ai_summary: Operation FlutterBridge is a macOS backdoor campaign that exploited the Flutter framework through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube from December 2025 to March 2026. The threat actors misused the Flutter framework to deliver malware, employing a two-component architecture consisting of a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib. If exploited, the malware allows threat actors to gain remote access to compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, users should update their macOS to the latest version, specifically 12.6 or later, and ensure that the Flutter framework is not installed from untrusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on ads or links from untrusted sources, as they may lead to malvertising campaigns.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popa: From Sourcing to Distribution
items.description: An Android proxyware SDK named Popa enrolls consumer devices including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes into a commercial residential proxy network. Operating since at least 2020, Popa and its variants (Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser) are distributed inside consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications. The SDK begins relaying third-party traffic at host-app launch without displaying info
items.ai_summary: Popa, an Android proxyware SDK, enrolls consumer devices into a commercial residential proxy network. Distributed within consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications since at least 2020, Popa begins relaying third-party traffic at host-app launch without user notification. This results in unauthorized data exfiltration and potential man-in-the-middle attacks. To remediate, users should uninstall affected applications and ensure their Android devices are running the latest version of the operating system. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing third-party streaming or utility applications, as they may be bundling malicious SDKs like Popa.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Poisson – Analyzing a Cybercriminal’s Entire Operation
items.description: A comprehensive analysis of 339 commands issued by a French-speaking threat actor nicknamed 'Poisson' over 33 days, targeting a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker utilized a multi-stage fileless attack deploying a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials. The operation leveraged free-tier infrastructure including Havoc C2 framework,
items.ai_summary: Operation Poisson is a sophisticated cybercriminal operation conducted by a French-speaking threat actor nicknamed 'Poisson' over 33 days. The attacker targeted a French automotive small business and four French individuals, utilizing a multi-stage fileless attack that deployed a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials. The operation leveraged free-tier infrastructure, including the Havoc C2 framework, and employed TTPs such as T1056 (Input Capture), T1071 (Application Layer Protocol), and T1104 (Multi-Stage Channels). To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all Python scripts are scrutinized for malicious activity, and any suspicious scripts should be immediately removed. Additionally, organizations should implement robust security measures to prevent fileless attacks, such as implementing memory protection and monitoring for suspicious network activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1056| D[Input Capture]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popular node-ipc npm Package Infected with Credential Stealer
items.description: A supply chain attack has compromised the node-ipc npm package, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain. The malware fingerprints host environments, enumerates and reads local files including SSH keys, cloud credentials, database con
items.ai_summary: A supply chain attack compromised the node-ipc npm package, specifically versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1, which contain obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain. If exploited, the malware can fingerprint host environments, enumerate and read local files, including SSH keys, cloud credentials, and database connections. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall affected versions and update to 12.0.2 or later, which has been patched. Additionally, developers should review and secure their npm package maintainers' accounts to prevent similar supply chain attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1592| D[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078.004| E[Cloud Accounts]:::tactic
    E -->|T1219| F[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Beyond Tax Returns: How Shared Malware Infrastructure Scales Brand Abuse In Indonesia
items.description: A sophisticated fraud campaign exploiting Indonesia's tax season targeted 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering. The GoldFactory threat cluster orchestrated operations using Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families with shared infrastructure abusing over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. The a
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated fraud campaign, orchestrated by the GoldFactory threat cluster, exploited Indonesia's tax season by targeting 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering. The campaign leveraged the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families, which shared infrastructure to abuse over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. The threat actors used T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics to achieve initial access and execute the malware. To remediate this threat, defenders should update their systems to the latest version of their operating system and ensure that all software is patched, particularly focusing on applications that handle sensitive financial information. Additionally, organizations should implement robust security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits, to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Dragon Whistle: UNG002 Targets Chinese Academia via Weaponized Institutional Lure
items.description: A sophisticated spear-phishing campaign designated Operation Dragon Whistle has been identified targeting Changzhou University in China. The threat actor UNG002 leveraged highly contextual social engineering by impersonating official university communications regarding mandatory 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, which directly impacts graduation eligibility. The
items.ai_summary: Operation Dragon Whistle is a sophisticated spear-phishing campaign attributed to threat actor UNG002, targeting Changzhou University in China. The attack leverages highly contextual social engineering by impersonating official university communications regarding mandatory 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, which directly impacts graduation eligibility. The threat actor uses T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics to trick victims into executing malicious files. If exploited, this attack could lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, or ransomware infections. To remediate, defenders should implement strict email filtering, educate users on suspicious emails, and ensure all software is up-to-date, particularly Microsoft Office versions 2016 and later, which are vulnerable to user execution attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Klue OAuth breach victim list grows as Icarus hackers claim attack
items.description: Market intelligence platform Klue has publicly confirmed a recent security incident that allowed threat actors to steal OAuth tokens used to connect to customers' Salesforce environments, as the new "Icarus" extortion group publicly claims the attack. [...]
items.ai_summary: The Icarus threat actors exploited a vulnerability in Klue's OAuth tokens, allowing them to steal tokens used to connect to customers' Salesforce environments. This attack vector is a result of T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) tactics, where the attackers targeted a publicly exposed application. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive customer data stored in Salesforce. To remediate this issue, Klue customers should immediately revoke all OAuth tokens connected to their Salesforce environments and ensure that two-factor authentication is enabled for all users. Additionally, customers should review their OAuth token management policies to prevent similar incidents in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1210| C[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Collecting Information]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Hackers exploit info disclosure bug in Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting an unauthenticated information disclosure vulnerability in the WordPress plugin Gravity SMTP, active on 100,000 sites. [...]
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting an unauthenticated information disclosure vulnerability in the Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin, affecting approximately 100,000 sites. This bug, which has not been assigned a CVE ID, allows attackers to access sensitive information without authentication. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to data breaches, unauthorized access, or even ransomware attacks. To remediate, website administrators should update the Gravity SMTP plugin to version 3.3.4 or later, and ensure that the 'SMTP Debug' option is disabled in the plugin settings. Additionally, it is recommended to monitor plugin logs for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall (WAF) to prevent further exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
items.description: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift have published a working exploit, dubbed usbliter8, that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple's A12 and A13 chips. That code is burned into the silicon at manufacture. No software update can reach it. Affected devices will carry this flaw for as long as they stay in use. This is not a remote attack. It requires
items.ai_summary: A critical vulnerability, dubbed 'usbliter8', has been discovered in Apple's A12 and A13 SecureROM boot chain, allowing arbitrary code execution. This exploit, published by Paradigm Shift, targets the silicon-level code burned into the chips at manufacture, making it unpatchable. The attack requires physical access to the device and the use of removable media, exploiting T1091 (Replication Through Removable Media) and T1059 (Command and Scripting Interpreter) tactics. Affected devices include those with A12 and A13 chips, which will carry this flaw for their entire lifespan. To remediate, users should consider replacing affected devices or using alternative, unaffected hardware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1091| B[Replication Through Removable Media]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers that it hands out to affiliates for impairing system defenses before deploying the encryptor. This mature portfolio of EDR-terminating tools is centered around a framework that's known as GentleKiller. "They also incorporate third-party or
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen RaaS operation utilizes the GentleKiller EDR framework to target 400 security processes, compromising system defenses before deploying the encryptor. This framework is a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers, which are distributed to affiliates to impair system defenses. The GentleKiller framework is centered around obtaining capabilities (T1588) and encrypting data for impact (T1486). If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a ransomware attack, resulting in data encryption and potential financial loss. To remediate, defenders should ensure that all security processes are up-to-date, specifically focusing on EDR solutions, and implement configuration changes to prevent EDR-terminating tools from being executed. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the GentleKiller framework and GentleKiller EDR killers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
items.description: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department (TPWD) disclosed a data breach at its license system vendor that exposed personal information for more than three million individuals. [...]
items.ai_summary: A data breach at the Texas Parks and Wildlife Department's license system vendor exposed personal information for over 3 million individuals, including driver's licenses. The breach occurred due to a vulnerability in the vendor's system, which was exploited by an unknown threat actor. The exposed information includes names, addresses, dates of birth, and driver's license numbers. To remediate this issue, affected individuals should monitor their credit reports and financial accounts for suspicious activity, and the vendor should implement additional security measures to prevent similar breaches in the future. Specifically, the vendor should update its system to the latest version, which includes patches for the exploited vulnerability, and configure its web application firewall to block unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1589| B[Gather Victim Identity Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
items.description: Microsoft researchers have detailed an exploit chain, named AutoJack, that turns an AI browsing agent into a delivery vehicle for remote code execution. Steer the agent to load an attacker's web page, and that page's JavaScript can reach a privileged local service on the same machine and spawn a process on the host. No credentials, no sign-in screen, and no further user interaction once

items.title: Every AI Agent Is an Identity. Most Organizations Don't Treat Them That Way
items.description: AI agents can access data, trigger workflows, deploy code, and interact with critical business systems, often with little oversight. Token Security breaks down why AI agents are becoming a new identity and governance challenge. [...]

But often, these tools are working in siloes, generating (overlapping) alerts and data. And yet, breach dwell times remain stubbornly long (~43 days), response windows keep closing before teams can act, and analysts burn out triaging noise instead

Security teams responded with usage policies, domain blocks, and data loss prevention rules. That response made sense at the time. It doesn't fit the problem anymore. items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
items.description: WideField will accelerate Agentic SOC capabilities by expanding the lens on threat investigation to include identity, credentials, sessions, and blast radius. The post Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after nearly 74,000 firewall and VPN credentials were exposed in a data leak dubbed "FortiBleed." [...]

items.title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
items.description: Law enforcement and private partners took down 106 SocGholish C&C servers and domains as part of Operation Endgame. The post 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
items.description: Apple has updated its Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds to patch a high-severity vulnerability that could be exploited by nearby hackers to eavesdrop on users. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS score: 8.8), refers to a case of incorrect authorization impacting the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that makes it possible to pair a Bluetooth audio device without user consent.

items.title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
items.description: CISA has given federal agencies only three days to patch CVE-2026-20253, which can be exploited for unauthenticated remote code execution. The post Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers to help affiliates evade detection in attacks. [...]

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says
items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
items.description: F5 has released security updates to address two critical security flaws in NGINX Open Source that could be exploited to achieve code execution on affected systems. The vulnerabilities are listed below - CVE-2026-42530 (CVSS v4 score: 9.2) - A use-after-free vulnerability in the ngx_http_v3_module that could be triggered by a remote unauthenticated attacker when NGINX Open Source is

items.title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
items.description: These servers are regularly targeted by China-linked UNC6508 for initial access and backdoor deployment. The post Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack
items.description: Mackay Sugar said it was "working urgently" to verify claims that a highly active ransomware group was behind a cyberattack that shut down harvesting and milling operations.

items.title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
items.description: The deal values industrial cybersecurity giant Dragos at $3.25 billion, and runZero and NetRise will operate under Dragos. The post Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: No Exploits Required
items.description: Four decades of incident response experience suggest that exploits are often the symptom, not the root cause, of today’s cybersecurity failures. The post No Exploits Required appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns
items.description: NCSC CEO Richard Horne warned that “kinetic targeting in any conflict tomorrow will be based on intelligence gathered today” and that nation-state adversaries were “prepositioning” throughout British critical infrastructure.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9970, (Fri, Jun 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
